findings.description: Table structure has been significantly altered, combining separate sections into one continuous layout and flattening hierarchical headers.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents the data in multiple distinct table sections (e.g., two sections on page 1). The HTML merges all years (1999 to 2014) into a single, extremely wide horizontal table. Additionally, the spanning column headers in the PDF (where a year spans over 'Amount' and 'Percent' columns) are flattened into single-level headers in the HTML (e.g., '1999-2000 Amount [$]').

findings.description: Loss of text formatting and indentation in the table's first column, removing visual hierarchy.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the 'Sources of revenue' column uses bold text (e.g., 'Income Taxes:', 'Total Tax Revenue') and indentation to indicate categories and sub-categories. The HTML rendering strips this formatting, presenting all items with uniform font weight and alignment, which obscures the hierarchical relationship of the data points.

findings.description: Text formatting (bolding and underlining) is missing in the footnotes section.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses bolding and underlining to highlight key terms at the beginning of footnote paragraphs (e.g., 'Estate Tax.', 'Soft Drink Tax.', '*Real Estate Conveyance Tax.'). The HTML renders these paragraphs as plain text without the distinguishing styles.

findings.description: The HTML document skips the <h1> heading level.
findings.evidence:
The document starts directly with `

TABLE 2. STATE GENERAL FUND: TAX REVENUES BY SOURCE

`, missing an initial

heading for the top-level document structure.
